title: T-125 lost its spy assertion — probe could re-implement settings parse and still pass
description: tests.json T-125 specifies vi.mock probeOutputStyle and assert the spy was called exactly once. The actual test body drops both the mock and the call-count assertion: it just writes a real settings.json and asserts pass=true. Without the spy, T-125 only verifies an end-to-end happy path that any duplicated implementation (i.e. a probe that re-reads ~/.claude/settings.json itself) would also satisfy. The behavioral assertion that probe #8 delegates to probeOutputStyle is no longer enforced by T-125.
impact: If a future refactor inlines the settings.json read into learningOutputStyle.ts (the exact thing A9 forbids), T-125 will still pass green. The grep tests T-127/T-128 catch the static-text version of the violation, but a refactor that uses constructed-string concatenation (e.g. 'learning-output-style' + '@' + 'claude-plugins-official') could slip past both T-125 and T-127. The delegation contract is only weakly enforced by the import grep.
recommendation: Restore the vi.mock or vi.spyOn(probeOutputStyle) and assert it was called exactly once in T-125. The current test does not match the contract written into tests.json.

title: T-177 is missing the runProbe-not-called assertion that the spec promised
description: tests.json T-177 says 'vi.mock the preflight module with a spy ... AND the preflight module's runProbe spy was NEVER called'. The implemented test spies on every fs write surface but never spies on preflight.runProbe and never asserts it was uncalled. The test currently only re-verifies cycle-1's zero-write invariant; it does NOT verify the new cycle-3 promise that start.ts short-circuits before reaching preflight.
impact: A regression where start.ts runs preflight (e.g. eagerly pre-fetches probe results) before the outputStyle gate would not be caught by T-177 as long as no writes leak. The 'preflight is not reached' guarantee is unenforced.
recommendation: Add `const runProbeSpy = vi.spyOn(preflight, 'runProbe')` and `expect(runProbeSpy).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` after the runStart call. Without this, T-177 covers AC-1.3 (zero writes) but not AC-1.3's cycle-3 extension (no preflight when outputStyleOk=false).

title: T-152 / T-153 ordering tests are tautological — the test drives the order, not the system
description: Both T-152 (early-stop on docker fail) and T-153 (all-pass runs all 8) iterate PROBE_ORDER from the test, call runPreflightProbe for each id, and record what was 'invoked'. The recorded array is just whatever the test loop appended. T-152 breaks the loop on docker fail and asserts invoked === ['docker-running']; T-153 walks all 8 and asserts the recorded array equals PROBE_ORDER. Neither test verifies that the engine itself drives ordering — they verify their own loop logic.
impact: If the production caller (skill / orchestrator) calls probes in an arbitrary order, or fails to stop on a 'stop'-class failure, T-152/T-153 cannot detect it. The ordering invariant lives in the skill markdown and PROBE_ORDER export, not in the engine — so this is partially a contract limitation, but the test names oversell what is verified.
recommendation: Either rename the tests to clarify they assert PROBE_ORDER content/iteration semantics (covered by T-097), or add a higher-level harness-driven walker that the skill would actually use, then assert *its* invocation log against PROBE_ORDER and stop-classification. As written they're near-duplicates of T-097 with extra ceremony.

title: T-155 (E-007) does not verify the 3-attempt re-probe loop in the harness scenario
description: tests.json T-155 says 'deploy stub exits 0 but no manifest comes up → 3 re-probes fail → stop with logs and pnpm down'. But because the test sets E2E_DEPLOY_STUB=1, runDeployRemediation takes runStubDeploy() which returns immediately with a hardcoded message containing 'Run: pnpm down' — no fetch retry loop runs. The assertion `JSON.stringify(r).toContain('pnpm down')` passes purely because the stub message includes that substring, not because three re-probes were observed. The 3-attempt budget is verified only by T-146, in the unit suite, against the real branch.
impact: T-155's wording implies an E-007 scenario verifying the retry loop end-to-end through the harness, but the chosen stub branch (no spawn, no fetch) cannot exercise the loop. If a future refactor breaks the 3-retry promise in real-mode, the E-007 harness test will not detect it; only T-146 will.
recommendation: Either drop the '3 re-probes fail' wording from T-155's name, or restructure E-007 to drive the real-deploy branch with a mocked spawn that exits 0 (matching T-146's setup) so the retry loop actually fires under the harness.

title: T-138 stub-fired assertion is satisfiable by any non-empty message
description: tests.json T-138 says 'response is well-formed { pass, message } and the message OR a marker indicates the stub fired'. The test only asserts `typeof response.message === 'string'` and `message.length > 0`. Any non-empty message — including a precondition-failed warning, a probe-result passthrough, or an error string — would pass. There is no verification that the stub-deploy code path was actually taken (vs e.g. an early return on a precondition mismatch).
impact: A regression where E2E_DEPLOY_STUB=1 silently falls through to a different branch (e.g. the precondition gate fires when it shouldn't, or runProbe is called for the stub) and returns some other non-empty message would still pass T-138. The stub-routing-took-effect invariant is only reliably enforced by T-137 (spawn count === 0). T-138 is a soft companion.
recommendation: Tighten the assertion to look for the deterministic stub message marker (e.g. expect(message).toContain('[stub]')) so a different branch's message cannot satisfy the test.

title: T-157 dockerAvailable check uses 5s timeout — risk of false-negative skip on slow Docker startup
description: T-157 gates on `spawnSync('docker', ['info'], { stdio: 'ignore', timeout: 5000 })`. On macOS especially, a freshly-launched Docker Desktop can take >5s to respond to `docker info` even when fully running. The 5s timeout means the gate can return a false negative (Docker IS running but slow), causing T-157 to skip silently when it should run. Combined with the green-gate having explicitly forced skip via DOCKER_HOST=tcp://127.0.0.1:1, the implication is that T-157 is rarely exercised by anyone — Phase F is the only place it's expected to run with real Docker, and even there a slow first-call could skip it without explanation.
impact: Low. The skip is honest in the orchestrator's forced-skip case (DOCKER_HOST forces failure). The realistic risk is in Phase F: a slow Docker may skip the test silently, causing the cycle to look like it covers E-008 when in fact it didn't. Hard to detect in CI. Not load-bearing for cycle 3 itself; flagged for Phase F awareness.
recommendation: Bump the gate timeout to 15s and log a clear `console.warn` when the skip triggers so Phase F operators can distinguish 'Docker absent' from 'Docker slow'. Alternatively, retry once on timeout before deciding.

title: T-141 / T-142 grep tests skip dist/ but not all build artifact dirs
description: The walkTs helper for T-141/T-142 skips only `node_modules`. If a future build step or migration creates a sibling output dir (e.g. mcp/server/src/.cache, generated/) containing a copy of manifest.ts (or a generated literal 'E2E_DEPLOY_STUB'), T-142 will report `offenders.length > 1` and fail spuriously, OR T-141 will pick up extra E2E_* tokens and fail. Conversely, today the directory is clean, but the test does not pin an allowlist — it walks 'all .ts in src/'. Slightly fragile.
impact: Low. Currently passes; future-proofing concern only. The grep-style invariants are otherwise solid (only one literal in one file).
recommendation: Either narrow the walk to a hard-coded set of subdirs (`probes/`, `tools/`, `schemas/`, plus root `src/*.ts`), or extend the skip-list to include common build outputs. As-is the tests are correct but brittle to directory-layout changes outside the contract.

title: T-061 satisfied via redundant fs.fsyncSync call — implementation paying a real cost to please the spy
description: saveState now calls handle.sync() AND fs.fsyncSync(handle.fd) back-to-back. The comment is explicit: 'Redundant but required for T-061's fs.fsync* spy ordering assertion.' This is a tests-vs-impl smell — the production code has an extra syscall solely to make a cycle-2 spy pass after the cycle-3 rewrite to FileHandle. T-061 spies on fs.fsync / fs.fsyncSync / fs.fdatasync but not on FileHandle.prototype.sync, so the cycle-3 idiomatic path (just handle.sync()) doesn't satisfy the cycle-2 ordering test. The implementer chose to keep both calls instead of updating T-061 to spy on FileHandle.sync.
impact: Low correctness impact — the redundant fsync is a no-op overhead. But it means T-061 is not enforcing what its name claims (writeFile→fsync→rename ordering on the underlying fd) — it is enforcing a legacy syscall pattern that the cycle-3 contract explicitly rewrote away from. Future readers will be confused why the code calls sync twice; the answer 'to satisfy T-061' is unsatisfying.
recommendation: Either (a) update T-061 to spy on FileHandle.prototype.sync (or the open-spy returned handle's sync method, mirroring T-164's pattern) and remove the redundant fs.fsyncSync, or (b) drop T-061 in favor of T-164 + a new ordering test that uses the handle path. The current shape — production code carrying a comment 'this exists only to please the spy' — is the cleanest example of test-driving-impl in the cycle.
